resume:
Dr. Eleanor Whitfield, MD, MBA
VP of Clinical Operations & Population Health

PROFESSIONAL SUMMARY
Physician-executive with over twenty years bridging frontline clinical care and enterprise operational leadership across integrated delivery networks, value-based care organizations, and digital health. I have led clinical operations for multi-site health systems, owned full operating budgets exceeding nine figures, and been accountable to boards for quality, access, cost, and patient safety outcomes. My orientation is relentlessly outcome-driven: I translate clinical strategy into measurable improvements in cost of care, readmission rates, patient experience, and clinician engagement. I am equally comfortable in a board room defending a capital request, in a value-based contracting negotiation with a major payer, and on a hospital floor conducting a root-cause analysis after a sentinel event.

PROFESSIONAL EXPERIENCE

Meridian Integrated Health — VP, Clinical Operations & Population Health (2019 to present)
Report to the Chief Medical Officer with a dotted line to the COO. Own clinical operations across fourteen ambulatory sites and two acute facilities, a combined operating budget in the low hundreds of millions, and a workforce of roughly 2,400 including employed physicians, advanced practice providers, nurses, and operational staff. Accountable to the quality committee of the board for the system's performance on CMS Stars, HEDIS measures, total cost of care under at-risk contracts, and patient safety.
- Led the system's transition into two-sided risk arrangements covering more than 180,000 attributed lives, building the clinical operating model for care management, risk stratification, and post-acute network steerage.
- Chaired the system-wide readmissions reduction program spanning cardiology, pulmonology, and general medicine service lines, coordinating across case management, pharmacy, and ambulatory follow-up.
- Sponsored the deployment of a remote patient monitoring program for heart failure and COPD patients, partnering with a digital health vendor and the informatics team on integration into the EHR.
- Rebuilt the clinician engagement and burnout-reduction agenda after a difficult post-pandemic period, owning the response to the annual provider experience survey.
- Negotiated quality and cost terms in value-based contracts with two regional payers, working alongside the contracting and finance teams.
- Restructured the ambulatory access model, redesigning templates, panel sizes, and same-day capacity across primary care.

Northgate Regional Medical Center — Senior Director, Quality & Care Transformation (2014 to 2019)
Led quality, accreditation readiness, and care-redesign initiatives for a 480-bed tertiary center. Owned the Joint Commission survey readiness program, the hospital's sepsis and CLABSI reduction bundles, and the length-of-stay reduction initiative across medicine and surgery.
- Directed the sepsis early-recognition program in partnership with the ED and ICU leadership and the informatics team.
- Co-led the surgical services throughput initiative addressing first-case on-time starts and OR turnover.
- Built the quality data infrastructure and the executive dashboard reviewed monthly by the operating committee.
- Managed the response and corrective-action planning for several sentinel events, including chairing root-cause analyses.

Lakeside Physician Group — Medical Director, Primary Care (2010 to 2014)
Provided clinical and operational leadership to a 60-provider multi-specialty group. Practiced internal medicine part-time while leading the group's early patient-centered medical home certification and its first foray into shared-savings contracting.
- Led PCMH recognition across all primary care sites.
- Introduced standardized chronic-disease management protocols for diabetes and hypertension.
- Served as physician champion for the EHR optimization effort.

EARLIER CAREER
Practiced as a board-certified internist in a hospital-employed setting for several years following residency, carrying a full panel and serving on the pharmacy and therapeutics committee. This frontline period grounds my operational work in the realities of clinical practice and remains central to how I lead.

EDUCATION & CREDENTIALS
- MBA, Health Sector Management, Fuqua School of Business, Duke University
- MD, University of Michigan Medical School
- BS, Biology, University of Wisconsin–Madison
- Board Certified, Internal Medicine (active)
- Certified Physician Executive (CPE)
- Graduate, system-sponsored Lean for Healthcare leadership program

SELECTED AREAS OF FOCUS
Value-based care operating models; two-sided risk and shared-savings contracting; population health and risk stratification; readmissions and total-cost-of-care reduction; quality and patient safety (CMS Stars, HEDIS, Joint Commission readiness); ambulatory access redesign; clinician engagement and burnout; EHR-enabled care management and remote patient monitoring; sentinel-event root-cause analysis and corrective action.

BOARDS & SERVICE
Serve on the clinical advisory committee of a regional health information exchange and have served as a peer reviewer for a clinical operations journal. Frequent invited speaker on operationalizing value-based care at regional health-system forums.

LEADERSHIP PHILOSOPHY
I lead from outcomes backward. In every role I start by asking what the board, the payers, and ultimately the patients are actually accountable for, then I build the operating model that makes those numbers move. I believe clinical operations leadership lives or dies on three things: a clear line of sight from frontline workflow to enterprise metric, disciplined measurement that survives audit, and the credibility with physicians to change how care is actually delivered rather than just issuing a policy. I am a firm believer that you cannot improve what you do not instrument, so in each organization I have prioritized building the data infrastructure and the governance cadence first. I run a monthly operating review, a weekly tiered huddle structure, and a quarterly business review with payer partners, and I expect every program owner to walk in with a numerator, a denominator, a trend line, and a clearly stated countermeasure when performance drifts.

SELECTED ENGAGEMENTS & INITIATIVES (DETAIL)
- Care management redesign: Rebuilt the embedded care-management model across the at-risk primary care panels, moving from a centralized telephonic team to an embedded, risk-stratified model with defined caseload ratios and standardized escalation pathways for rising-risk and high-risk cohorts. Partnered with pharmacy on medication reconciliation at every transition of care.
- Post-acute network steerage: Designed the preferred skilled-nursing-facility network and the steerage protocol, aligning discharge planning with length-of-stay and readmission performance, and instituting a shared scorecard reviewed jointly with the post-acute partners.
- Behavioral health integration: Sponsored the integration of behavioral health into primary care through a collaborative-care model, recognizing the outsized impact of untreated behavioral health on total cost of care in the at-risk population.
- Annual wellness visit and gap-closure campaign: Owned the operational engine for annual wellness visits, HEDIS gap closure, and accurate risk-adjustment documentation, building the daily worklists and the provider-facing prompts inside the EHR.
- Telehealth standup: Led the rapid expansion of virtual care during the pandemic disruption and the subsequent rationalization of which visit types remained virtual based on outcome and access data.

WHAT COLLEAGUES SAY I DO WELL
I am most often recognized for translating ambiguous strategy into a concrete operating plan with owners and dates, for staying calm and structured in a crisis or a sentinel-event review, and for earning the trust of skeptical physicians by showing up on the floor and engaging with the real workflow rather than managing from a dashboard alone. I hold a high bar for evidence and I am candid about what worked and what did not.
jd: A growing value-based care organization is seeking a Vice President of Clinical Operations to own the clinical operating model across a network of risk-bearing primary care clinics and a population of more than 200,000 attributed lives. Reporting to the Chief Medical Officer, this executive will be accountable to the board for total cost of care, medical loss ratio, readmission rates, CMS Star ratings, and patient and clinician experience. The role owns the operating budget for care management, the post-acute steerage strategy, and the clinical components of payer contracting. We require a physician-executive with a demonstrated, quantified track record of reducing total cost of care and readmissions in at-risk populations, deep fluency in the actual mechanics and math of value-based contracts, and the credibility to present outcomes to a board quality committee and to payer partners. The successful candidate will be able to walk through, with specific and verifiable numbers, exactly how they personally drove a cost or quality outcome, over what timeframe, and what tradeoffs they made. Prior experience standing up two-sided risk arrangements and remote patient monitoring at scale is strongly preferred.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at Meridian — sites, budget, lives, and the metrics you own.
You list a transition into two-sided risk for 180,000 lives. What was your specific role versus the contracting team's?
Let's talk total cost of care. Pick one at-risk population and tell me the baseline PMPM.
What did that PMPM move to, and over how many months?
How much of that movement was your clinical program versus secular trend or favorable risk coding?
Tell me about the readmissions program. What was the baseline 30-day rate by service line?
What did it land at, and what was the denominator?
Which single intervention in that bundle moved the rate the most?
The remote patient monitoring program — how many patients were enrolled, and what was the enrollment rate of eligible patients?
Did RPM actually reduce admissions, and how did you isolate that effect from the rest of the bundle?
On the payer contracts — what were the actual quality gates and the shared-savings split you negotiated?
What was the medical loss ratio before and after on that book of business?
Tell me about a tradeoff you made — somewhere you chose cost over access, or vice versa.
Walk me through the clinician burnout work. What did the provider experience score start at?
What specifically did you change, and what did the score become?
Let's go back to cost of care. You said you saved a lot — give me the absolute dollar figure and the attributed-lives base it was spread across.
Who validated that savings number — your finance team, the payer's actuary, or an external party?

I want to pin this down. You just said you 'took out forty percent of total cost of care in the first year' — for a 200,000-life book, that's an extraordinary claim. Where exactly did that forty percent come from, line by line?

candidate_last_answer: Look, I stand by the number — we drove out forty percent of total cost of care in that first year, and frankly I think it was closer to forty-five once you account for the downstream avoidance. It was transformational, honestly one of the largest swings anyone in the region has produced. Where did it come from? Everywhere, really — the care management model, the RPM program, the post-acute steerage, the access redesign, all of it compounding. We saved tens of millions, easily north of eighty million dollars, and the clinical engagement scores went through the roof at the same time. I don't have the line-by-line in front of me, but every program we touched delivered outsized returns. When you align the whole operating model the way we did, these numbers just follow.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: north of eighty million dollars

expected-yield: a line-by-line decomposition of the claimed 40% / $80M savings into specific program contributions tied to the attributed-lives base and a per-member-per-month figure, exposing whether the headline number is mathematically coherent or an inflated aggregate
